title: Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center CVE-2026-20131 (CVSS 10.0): Unauthenticated Java Deserialization RCE Exploited as Zero-Day Since January 2026 — Interlock Ransomware Achieves Root Access 36 Days Before Patch

sources.name: AWS Security Blog - Amazon Threat Intelligence Teams Identify Interlock Ransomware Campaign
BleepingComputer - Interlock Ransomware Exploited Secure FMC Flaw in Zero-Day Attacks Since January
The Hacker News - Interlock Ransomware Exploits Cisco FMC Zero-Day CVE-2026-20131 for Root Access
Help Net Security - Cisco FMC flaw was exploited by Interlock weeks before patch
eSentire - Cisco Vulnerability CVE-2026-20131 Exploited by Interlock
CSO Online - Ransomware group exploited Cisco firewall vulnerability as a zero day, weeks before a patch appeared
SecPod Blog - Interlock Group Leveraging Cisco FMC Flaw CVE-2026-20131 Weeks Before Public Disclosure
SecurityOnline - Exploited in the Wild: Interlock Ransomware Weaponizes Critical 10.0 CVSS Cisco Zero-Day

timeline.event: Interlock ransomware group begins exploiting CVE-2026-20131 as a zero-day, 36 days before public disclosure; AWS MadPot honeypot network first detects exploitation activity
Cisco publicly discloses CVE-2026-20131 (CVSS 10.0) and releases patched versions; confirms insecure Java deserialization allows unauthenticated RCE as root
AWS threat intelligence team publishes report confirming Interlock exploitation campaign; reveals zero-day exploitation window dating back to January 26
Multiple security vendors publish attack chain analysis; deployment of ConnectWise ScreenConnect, Volatility, and Certify as post-exploitation tools confirmed
CISA adds CVE-2026-20131 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog; federal agencies ordered to patch by April 9, 2026
Full post-exploitation chain published: PowerShell reconnaissance, ZIP archive exfiltration, double-extortion ransomware deployment confirmed

simple_summary.what_happened: A perfect-10 severity vulnerability was discovered in Cisco's Secure Firewall Management Center (FMC), the central management console used by organizations to control their Cisco firewall fleets. The flaw — caused by insecure deserialization of Java data — allowed any attacker on the internet to send a specially crafted request to the FMC web interface and immediately gain full root-level control without needing a username or password. Critically, the Interlock ransomware group was actively exploiting this vulnerability for 36 days before Cisco publicly disclosed it, meaning defenders had no awareness that their firewall management systems were being compromised throughout late January and all of February 2026.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any organization running an internet-accessible Cisco Secure Firewall Management Center on versions 6.4.0, 7.0.x through 7.7.x, or 10.0.0. FMC is a centralized management platform used in medium-to-large enterprise, government, and critical infrastructure environments that operate Cisco Secure Firewall (formerly Firepower) appliances. Organizations that have not yet patched to the fixed versions (7.0.9, 7.2.11, 7.4.6, 7.6.5, 7.7.12, or 10.0.1) are at immediate risk of ransomware deployment.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Interlock identifies target organizations running internet-exposed Cisco Secure FMC instances using Shodan or similar network scanning, then sends a crafted HTTP request containing a malicious serialized Java object to the FMC web management interface — requiring no credentials whatsoever. Step 2: The FMC deserializes the Java object, which triggers arbitrary code execution as the root user; the compromised system sends a callback HTTP PUT request to the attacker's server to confirm successful exploitation. Step 3: The attacker fetches and executes an ELF binary payload from a remote C2 server, establishing persistent root-level access to the firewall management platform. Step 4: ConnectWise ScreenConnect (a commercial remote desktop tool) is deployed to provide stable, persistent remote access that blends with legitimate IT traffic; web shells are also planted for redundant access. Step 5: A PowerShell-based reconnaissance script systematically collects system information, installed software, running services, browser data, active network connections, and Active Directory data — organizing everything into per-host directories on a centralized share and compressing them into ZIP archives for exfiltration. Step 6: Interlock leverages its control over the firewall management system to pivot throughout the network, disables security monitoring, and deploys ransomware across the enterprise in a double-extortion attack, simultaneously encrypting files and threatening to publish stolen data.
what_you_should_do: Apply Cisco's patches immediately: upgrade to FMC 7.0.9, 7.2.11, 7.4.6, 7.6.5, 7.7.12, or 10.0.1 depending on your version — Cisco has confirmed there are NO workarounds for this vulnerability

Assume compromise if FMC was internet-accessible since January 26, 2026: conduct a thorough incident response investigation before patching, as patching an already-compromised system does not remove existing backdoors
Immediately audit FMC systems for unauthorized ConnectWise ScreenConnect installations, unexpected web shells, or unusual outbound connections to external C2 servers
Restrict FMC web management interface access to internal management networks only using firewall rules; FMC should never be directly internet-accessible
Review all firewall policy changes made since January 26, 2026 for unauthorized modifications that may have been used to facilitate lateral movement
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: A CVSS 10.0 score is the highest possible and is reserved for vulnerabilities with unauthenticated, network-accessible, zero-interaction exploitability with maximum impact — this vulnerability meets all those criteria. The 36-day zero-day exploitation window is especially alarming because organizations actively patching their environments using standard vulnerability management cycles had no way of knowing their firewall management systems were being targeted. FMC is uniquely dangerous as a target because compromising it gives attackers centralized control over an organization's entire firewall fleet — an attacker who controls FMC can silently disable security policies, create traffic bypass rules, and use the trusted management network to pivot across the entire enterprise without triggering conventional security alerts.

tactics.description: Interlock sends crafted HTTP requests containing malicious serialized Java objects to the Cisco FMC web management interface, achieving unauthenticated root RCE via CVE-2026-20131
Java deserialization gadget chain executes arbitrary OS commands as root through the JVM runtime environment on the FMC appliance
Web shells planted on compromised FMC instances provide redundant persistent access independent of the initial deserialization exploit
ConnectWise ScreenConnect deployed on compromised FMC systems to maintain stable remote access that blends with legitimate commercial software traffic
PowerShell-based reconnaissance script collects system information, installed software, running services, browser data, and active connections from all reachable hosts
Reconnaissance data organized into per-host directories on centralized network shares, compressed into ZIP archives before exfiltration
Interlock deploys ransomware across the enterprise network after lateral movement, conducting double-extortion by combining file encryption with threat to publish stolen data

detection_guidance.summary: Detection should focus on three areas: identifying exploitation attempts via crafted deserialization requests to the FMC web interface, detecting post-exploitation artifacts (ConnectWise ScreenConnect, web shells, PowerShell reconnaissance activity), and identifying the callback HTTP PUT request that confirms successful exploitation. Organizations should also review FMC audit logs for unexpected configuration changes, particularly any modifications to firewall policies made since January 26, 2026.

log_sources.source: Cisco FMC Web Server / Audit Logs
log_sources.description: Monitor for anomalous HTTP POST requests to the FMC management interface containing large serialized Java payloads, and outbound HTTP PUT requests to external IP addresses immediately following inbound management traffic
Detect outbound connections from FMC appliance to unexpected external IP addresses, particularly ELF binary downloads and C2 callback traffic; FMC should have minimal outbound internet access
Monitor for unexpected process creation on FMC, particularly PowerShell execution, ConnectWise ScreenConnect installer execution, or ELF binary execution from temporary directories
Review all firewall policy modifications since January 26, 2026 for unauthorized changes that may have been made to facilitate attacker lateral movement
Monitor for unusual AD enumeration activity, ADCS certificate requests from non-standard principals (indicating Certify tool use), and new admin account creation from FMC network segments

what_to_look_for.indicator: Outbound HTTP PUT request from FMC to external IP immediately after inbound management request
what_to_look_for.description: Cisco's own advisory describes a callback HTTP PUT to an external server as the confirmation step following successful exploitation — this is a nearly unique and high-fidelity indicator
what_to_look_for.log_field: src_ip=<FMC_IP> AND http.method='PUT' AND dst_ip NOT IN <internal_ranges>

what_to_look_for.indicator: ConnectWise ScreenConnect installation on FMC appliance
what_to_look_for.description: Interlock deploys ScreenConnect as a persistence mechanism; presence of ScreenConnect on a Cisco FMC is almost certainly malicious as FMC is a managed network appliance, not a general-purpose workstation

what_to_look_for.indicator: PowerShell execution spawning from FMC web service processes
what_to_look_for.description: Post-exploitation reconnaissance uses PowerShell scripts; PowerShell execution originating from FMC web service process trees indicates active post-compromise activity
what_to_look_for.log_field: process.name='powershell.exe' AND process.parent contains 'fmc' OR process.parent contains 'java'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Large HTTP POST to FMC management interface containing Java serialization magic bytes
what_to_look_for.description: Java serialization payloads begin with hex bytes AC ED 00 05; requests to the FMC web interface containing these bytes in the body are exploitation attempts
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.method='POST' AND dst_ip=<FMC_IP> AND http.request.body hex contains 'aced0005'

immediate_actions.action: Apply Cisco FMC patches immediately — no workarounds exist
immediate_actions.description: Upgrade to the appropriate fixed version for your FMC branch: 7.0.9, 7.2.11, 7.4.6, 7.6.5, 7.7.12, or 10.0.1. There are NO workarounds or mitigations — patching is the only supported remediation

immediate_actions.action: Restrict FMC network access before patching
immediate_actions.description: Immediately block all internet access to the FMC web management interface (TCP 443, TCP 8305) using perimeter firewall rules; FMC should only be accessible from dedicated management network segments
immediate_actions.command: iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -s <management_subnet> -j ACCEPT && iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j DROP

immediate_actions.action: Investigate for compromise if FMC was internet-exposed since January 26, 2026
immediate_actions.description: Patching a compromised system does not remove backdoors. Conduct forensic investigation: check for ConnectWise ScreenConnect, unusual processes, web shells, and unexpected outbound connections before patching

immediate_actions.action: Audit all FMC policy changes since January 26, 2026
immediate_actions.description: Review Cisco FMC audit logs for any unauthorized firewall policy modifications, new administrator account creation, or policy bypass rules added during the zero-day exploitation window

immediate_actions.action: Hunt for Certify and AD CS abuse indicators
immediate_actions.description: Interlock deploys the Certify tool to target Active Directory Certificate Services for privilege escalation and persistence; audit AD CS for unusual certificate template modifications or unexpected certificate enrollments
